text: Here we discuss our method of excising difficult-to-remove gastric cysts or gastrointestinal stromal tumors. These tumors are often located on the lesser curve, posterior wall, and oricardia of the stomach. They can be endo or exophytic.
Robotic surgery provides an ergonomic benefit over standard laparoscopy to access these types of GISTs because they are not amenable to stapled wedge resection.

Here we demonstrate our technique for robotic resection of an endophytic posterior gastric GIST located at the cardia. We have no disclosures. Here we show representative CT slices of a patient who presented with a posterior wall GIST in the cardia of the stomach. We will demonstrate how a tumor such as this can be removed using a robotic approach.

The surgeon performs an intraoperative esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) to confirm the intragastric tumor location, facilitating the selection of an appropriate gastrotomy site. Careful dissection along the lesser curve of the stomach is conducted to achieve adequate mobilization for the gastrotomy.

It is also important to note that the tumor must be stapled completely beneath the stalk, as this will allow for negative margins on pathology. Closure of the gastronomy site involves an imbricated two-layer closure.
The surgeon conducts a leak test under direct visualization, using a repeat EGD to verify that the gastronomy repair site is free of leakage.

The video shows the port placements for the robotic surgery, including five ports with one designated as a bedside assistant port. An additional liver retractor is visible. The robotic arms are configured with a fenestrated bipolar grasper in the first arm, a laparoscopic robotic camera in the second arm, an alternating electrocautery hook, vessel sealer, or robotic stapler in the third arm, and caudate forceps in the fourth arm.

The surgeon performs an intraoperative EGD to confirm the gastrotomy site placement, revealing the tumor within the gastric lumen. Subsequently, the gastrotomy site is created using hook electrocautery.

The laparoscopic camera is inserted into the gastrotomy site to visualize the intragastric tumor, confirming its placement. The gastrotomy site is then enlarged to facilitate the elevation and stapled resection of the tumor.

The surgeon enlarges the gastrotomy site to facilitate the mobilization of the gastric GIST. The tumor is elevated, allowing the stapler to pass underneath the stalk, ensuring negative margins for pathology. The close proximity of the lesser curve vasculature is noted during this process.

The surgeon closes the gastrotomy site using a horizontal running V-lock suture, followed by a vertical running V-lock suture for a second imbricated layer. An intraoperative EGD is performed to examine the suture layers from inside the stomach, confirming a negative leak test.

The excised posterior wall gastric gist is displayed. The surgical process involved confirming the tumor location with an EGD, careful dissection for tumor mobilization, stapling below the tumor base for R-zero resection, and a two-layer gastrotomy closure followed by a negative leak test.
